content.assessment: This proposal targets the specific fiscal-incidence mechanism identified in the evidence base: access to selective tracks depends partly on private exam preparation rather than ability, even controlling for prior scores (F-live-education_finance-03, F-live-education_finance-06). Reforming admissions without restructuring tracks is comparatively low-cost from a public-budget perspective, since it does not require reallocating per-pupil funding streams or restructuring school networks. However, its value for money is limited if the underlying incentive—private household investment in exam preparation—persists regardless of admissions procedure, since families with resources can adapt strategy to any procedural design.
opportunities: Could be paired with fiscal conditionality (as in a funding-lever approach) to reinforce admissions changes with resource incentives
strengths: Directly addresses the documented SES-linked admissions bias operating independently of prior test scores (F-live-education_finance-03)
Lower fiscal magnitude than structural reform, since it does not require reallocating funding streams or restructuring the school network
threats: If administration reform fails to alter the underlying private-preparation market, the public sector bears reform implementation costs without corresponding equity returns, weakening value for money
weaknesses: Does not address the underlying incentive created by identical per-pupil funding regardless of the private-preparation-dependent admission pathway (F-live-education_finance-06)
Private preparation access as a driver of inequity may simply relocate to different admissions criteria rather than being eliminated, given no change to household-level financial incentives
